tensions.name: Henotheism vs. Monotheism
Universal compassion vs. Tribal exclusivism
Omniscient sovereign vs. Fickle deity
Justice as moral order vs. Arbitrary divine action
Prophetic ethics vs. Priestly ritual
God as source of all vs. Evil externalized
Death as oblivion vs. Resurrection hope
Women as property vs. Women as agents
Davidic idealization vs. David's actual record
Conditional covenant vs. Unconditional promise
Free will vs. Divine determinism
Centralized vs. Distributed worship
Prophet vs. King vs. Priest authority
Election as privilege vs. Election as burden
God as warrior vs. God as shepherd
Historical memory vs. Mythological narrative
Law as eternal vs. Law as evolving
Individual vs. Collective responsibility
Land as gift vs. Land as conquest
Wisdom accessible vs. Wisdom unknowable

GEN.name: Genesis
GEN.dating: Composite: J (~950 BCE), E (~850 BCE), P (~550-450 BCE). Final redaction ~5th c. BCE.
GEN.sources: J, E, P interweaved. J and E dominate patriarchal narratives; P provides genealogies and ch.1 creation.
GEN.summary: Composite origin narrative combining at least three traditions. Moves from universal prehistory to one family's story, establishing covenant theology and territorial claims. Heavy ANE borrowing (Enuma Elish, Gilgamesh, Atrahasis).

T01.note: Strongly henotheistic: divine council ('let us'), sons of God, El names. Other gods assumed real.

T02.note: Both: blessing 'all families of earth' through Abraham, but also tribal origin stories justifying enmities.

T03.note: God walks in garden not knowing where Adam is, regrets creating humanity, negotiates with Abraham over Sodom.

T04.note: Both: Joseph's providential arc ('God meant it for good') vs. arbitrary flood, Sodom destruction, Isaac binding.

T05.note: Minimal: patriarchal altars (distributed ritual) but no prophetic tradition yet.

T06.note: God causes/permits all: sends flood, destroys Sodom, tests Abraham. No Satan figure. Serpent is an animal.

T07.note: Death as oblivion: 'go down to Sheol mourning.' No afterlife concept whatsoever.

T08.note: Mostly property (wife-sister deceptions, Hagar as vessel) but Rebekah and Tamar exercise real agency.

T10.note: Abrahamic covenant has conditions (circumcision) but Noahic is unconditional. Mixed.

T11.note: Both: human choice matters (Cain, Babel) but God also predetermines (choosing Jacob over Esau before birth).

T12.note: Fully distributed: patriarchs build altars anywhere, no central sanctuary concept.

T13.note: Pre-institutional: no prophets, kings, or priests as established roles.

T14.note: Election as privilege: chosen family receives land, descendants, divine protection.

T15.note: Both: God destroys (flood, Sodom) and protects (Hagar, Joseph).

T16.note: Heavily mythological: creation, talking serpent, Nephilim, flood, Babel, 900-year lifespans.

T17.note: Pre-law period: patriarchs practice what later law prohibits (sibling marriage, pillar worship).

T18.note: Mostly collective: flood kills all, Sodom destroyed collectively, family/tribal identity dominant.

T19.note: Land as divine gift/promise. No conquest yet — just promise.

conduct.diet: Post-flood: 'every moving thing that lives shall be food' (9:3), but not blood (9:4). No clean/unclean distinction yet.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Polygamy normal (Abraham, Jacob, Esau). Concubinage accepted (Hagar, Bilhah, Zilpah). Sibling marriage practiced without condemnation (Abraham-Sarah). Levirate-like duty (Tamar-Judah). Intermarriage with foreigners occurs freely.
conduct.homosexuality: Sodom narrative (ch.19) involves attempted gang rape of male visitors — about violence and hospitality violation, not consensual acts. No legislation.
conduct.slavery: Slavery normalized: Hagar is Sarah's slave. Joseph sold into slavery. Egyptian slavery as origin story. No regulation yet.
conduct.clothing: No regulations. Nakedness/clothing as shame/innocence marker (Eden narrative).
conduct.agriculture: No regulations. Cain/Abel as farmer/herder archetypes.
conduct.economic_justice: No formal codes. Joseph's grain management could be read as state economic planning or exploitation.
conduct.violence_war: Simeon and Levi's massacre of Shechem condemned by Jacob. Blood vengeance assumed (Cain's mark as protection). No formal war codes.
conduct.ritual_calendar: No formal calendar. Patriarchal altar-building at significant sites. Circumcision established as covenant sign (ch.17).

EXO.name: Exodus
EXO.dating: Composite: J, E (10th-9th c.), P (6th-5th c.). No archaeological evidence for exodus at described scale.
EXO.sources: J, E, P interweaved. Song of the Sea (ch.15) possibly oldest Hebrew poetry. P dominates tabernacle (chs.25-40).
EXO.summary: Foundational liberation narrative combined with covenant law establishment. Transitions from narrative (1-18) to legal/ritual material (19-40). Plagues assert YHWH's supremacy over Egyptian gods.

T01.note: 'Who is like you among the gods?' (15:11). 'No other gods before me' assumes others exist. Henotheistic throughout.

T02.note: Liberation narrative is particular to Israel; 'mixed multitude' joins but laws focus on Israelite community.

T03.note: God hardens Pharaoh's heart then punishes him. God nearly kills Moses (4:24). God changes mind after Moses argues (32:14).

T04.note: Plagues have retributive logic but hardened-heart problem undermines moral coherence. Book of Covenant shows rational case law.

T05.note: Both present: Moses as proto-prophet, but second half is dominated by priestly tabernacle instructions.

T06.note: God directly causes all: plagues, death of firstborn, hardening hearts, sending angel of death. No external evil agent.

T07.note: No afterlife concepts. Rewards/punishments entirely this-worldly.

T08.note: Women mostly background. Miriam has leadership role briefly. Midwives defy Pharaoh (rare female agency). Slave laws treat women as property.

T10.note: Sinai covenant heavily conditional: obedience brings blessing, disobedience brings curse. Suzerainty treaty form.

T11.note: Tension: 'Choose to obey' but God hardens hearts. People choose golden calf freely but God predetermined plague response.

T12.note: Moving toward centralization: tabernacle as single worship center, but not yet fixed location.

T13.note: Moses dominates as prophet-lawgiver. Aaron's priestly role established. Tension between them visible (golden calf).

T14.note: Both: liberation is privilege, but covenant obligations are heavy burden. 'Stiff-necked people' constantly failing.

T15.note: Heavily warrior: plagues, Red Sea drowning, 'man of war' (15:3). Some shepherd imagery (leading through wilderness).

T16.note: Mix: plagues have mythological scale, sea splitting is cosmic. But Book of Covenant is practical law, not myth.

T17.note: Law presented as eternal divine revelation at Sinai. No acknowledgment of evolution.

T18.note: Heavily collective: entire Egypt punished, entire Israel covenanted, Achan-like collective liability assumed.

T19.note: Land as promised destination. Conquest not yet begun but anticipated.

conduct.diet: Passover meal regulations (ch.12). No systematic dietary code yet, but blood prohibition continues.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Slave marriage regulations (21:2-11). Seduction of unbetrothed virgin requires marriage or bride-price (22:16-17).
conduct.slavery: Detailed slave law (21:1-11): Hebrew slaves freed after 6 years. Ear-piercing for permanent servitude. Female slaves have different (lesser) rights. Beating slaves permitted if they survive (21:20-21).
conduct.clothing: Priestly garments specified in elaborate detail (ch.28). No general clothing regulations.
conduct.agriculture: Sabbath year: land fallow every 7th year (23:10-11). Firstfruits offerings.
conduct.economic_justice: No interest on loans to the poor (22:25). Return pledged cloak by sunset (22:26-27). No perverting justice for the poor or favoring the poor in lawsuits (23:3,6).
conduct.violence_war: Lex talionis: 'eye for eye, tooth for tooth' (21:24). Cities of refuge not yet established. Capital offenses include murder, striking parents, kidnapping.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Passover/Unleavened Bread, Feast of Harvest (Weeks), Feast of Ingathering (Booths) established (23:14-17). Sabbath commandment.

LEV.name: Leviticus
LEV.dating: Primarily P source, 6th-5th c. BCE. Holiness Code (chs.17-26, 'H') possibly slightly earlier.
LEV.sources: Almost entirely P, with Holiness Code (H) as distinct sub-source.
LEV.summary: Priestly operating manual: sacrifice, purity, holiness. Post-exilic priestly class establishing authority. Holiness Code mixes ritual and ethical commands.

T01.note: YHWH assumed supreme but Azazel (scapegoat demon?) hints at other supernatural beings. Focus on YHWH-only worship.

T02.note: Mostly internal regulations, but 'love the stranger as yourself' (19:34) alongside strict separation.

T03.note: God as systematic, predictable: follow rules, maintain purity, receive blessing. Orderly divine nature.

T04.note: Strongly moral-order: precise cause-effect between ritual compliance and divine favor.

T05.note: Maximally priestly: entire book is ritual regulation. Prophetic ethics appear only in Holiness Code interjections.

T06.note: God is source of blessing/curse. Azazel hints at externalized evil but within God's system.

T07.note: Death is contamination source, not gateway. No afterlife. Corpse contact produces impurity.

T08.note: Women primarily subjects of purity regulation (menstruation, childbirth). Valued less in vow-redemption prices (27:1-7).

T10.note: Highly conditional: blessings and curses in ch.26 directly tied to obedience.

T11.note: Strong free-will assumption: people choose to follow or violate purity codes.

T12.note: Maximally centralized: all sacrifice at the tabernacle. Slaughter outside is a capital offense (17:3-4).

T13.note: Priestly authority absolute. Priests control all access to God through sacrifice and purity rulings.

T14.note: Both: holiness is privilege ('be holy as I am holy') but also heavy burden of constant ritual compliance.

T15.note: God mostly absent as character; functions as rule-giver. Neither warrior nor shepherd here.

T16.note: Entirely legal/regulatory. No narrative, no myth. Pure institutional document.

T17.note: Laws presented as eternal, divine, unchangeable Sinai revelation.

T18.note: Both: individual sin offerings for personal transgressions, but communal Day of Atonement for collective guilt.

T19.note: Wilderness setting; land not a focus.

conduct.diet: Most detailed dietary code: clean/unclean animals specified (ch.11). No pork, shellfish, most insects, various birds. Blood and fat prohibited. Mary Douglas's boundary theory applies.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Extensive incest prohibitions (ch.18). No sex during menstruation (18:19). Adultery punishable by death (20:10). Bestiality prohibited (18:23).
conduct.homosexuality: Male same-sex acts prohibited: 'You shall not lie with a male as with a woman; it is an abomination' (18:22). Death penalty specified (20:13). Female same-sex acts never mentioned.
conduct.slavery: Jubilee provisions: Hebrew slaves freed in 50th year. Foreign slaves may be held permanently as property (25:44-46). Clear two-tier system.
conduct.clothing: No garment of mixed fibers — wool and linen together prohibited (19:19).
conduct.agriculture: No sowing field with two kinds of seed (19:19). Sabbath year (25:1-7). Jubilee year: land returns to original owners (25:8-55) — probably never practiced.
conduct.economic_justice: No interest to fellow Israelites (25:36-37). Gleaning rights: leave edges of fields for poor (19:9-10, 23:22). Fair weights and measures (19:35-36). 'Love your neighbor as yourself' (19:18).
conduct.violence_war: Capital offenses expanded: blasphemy, Molech worship, mediums, adultery, incest, bestiality, homosexual acts. Lex talionis restated (24:19-20).
conduct.ritual_calendar: Full festival calendar (ch.23): Sabbath, Passover, Unleavened Bread, Firstfruits, Weeks, Trumpets, Day of Atonement, Booths. Day of Atonement ritual detailed (ch.16).

NUM.name: Numbers
NUM.dating: Composite: J, E, P. Final form ~5th c. BCE.
NUM.sources: P dominates (census, priestly regulations). J/E provide wilderness narratives. Balaam oracles possibly very early.
NUM.summary: Wilderness wandering as testing and failure. Structured around two censuses. Rebellion narratives serve to cement Mosaic/Aaronic authority.

T01.note: Balaam's oracles assume YHWH is one god among others. Henotheistic warfare framework.

T02.note: Tribal: herem against Midianites (ch.31), total destruction commanded. Balaam serves YHWH but is non-Israelite.

T03.note: God responsive to Moses' arguments, changes plans. But also sends plagues impulsively after complaints.

T04.note: Punishments wildly disproportionate: entire generation condemned for spies' report. Moses barred from land for striking rock.

T05.note: Both: Levitical regulations alongside Moses as prophetic figure.

T06.note: God directly sends fiery serpents, plagues, earth-swallowing. No external evil agent.

T07.note: No afterlife. Death is simply death.

T08.note: Daughters of Zelophehad win inheritance rights (ch.27) — rare female legal victory. But conditioned on marrying within tribe (ch.36).

T10.note: Conditional: disobedience leads to 40 years wandering.

T11.note: People choose rebellion repeatedly, but God also determines outcomes.

T12.note: Tabernacle as center. Korah's challenge to priestly monopoly violently suppressed.

T13.note: Moses (prophetic) and Aaron (priestly) share authority. Korah's challenge to both is destroyed.

T14.note: Election increasingly burden: constant testing, punishment, death in wilderness.

T15.note: Heavily warrior: conquest begins, enemies destroyed, YHWH fights for Israel.

T16.note: Mix: talking donkey (Balaam), magical bronze serpent alongside census lists and route itineraries.

T17.note: Laws presented as Sinai/wilderness revelation.

T18.note: Heavily collective: entire generation punished, Korah's family destroyed, plague kills thousands for others' sins.

T19.note: Both: land promised but conquest of Transjordan begins. Gift and military action merge.

conduct.diet: No new dietary laws. Manna as divine provision.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Intermarriage with Moabites leads to Baal Peor crisis (ch.25). Midianite women killed but virgins kept (ch.31). Zelophehad's daughters must marry within tribe.
conduct.slavery: No new regulations.
conduct.clothing: Fringes/tassels commanded on garments as reminder of commandments (15:38-39).
conduct.economic_justice: No new codes.
conduct.violence_war: Herem warfare: Midianite massacre (ch.31) — kill all males and non-virgin women, keep virgin girls. Cities of refuge established (ch.35). Blood avenger system codified.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Offerings calendar detailed (chs.28-29): daily, sabbath, monthly, and festival offerings specified.

DEU.name: Deuteronomy
DEU.dating: Core ~7th c. BCE (Josiah's reform). Expanded during exile. 'Book found in temple' (2 Kings 22) widely identified as early Deuteronomy.
DEU.sources: D (Deuteronomist) source. Frame narrative may be exilic additions.
DEU.summary: 7th-century reform document retrojected onto Moses. Centralizes worship, establishes conditional covenant theology that becomes the interpretive framework for the Deuteronomistic History.

T01.note: Shema ('YHWH is one') is transitional: demands exclusive loyalty. 'God of gods' language still henotheistic. Moving toward monotheism.

T02.note: Both: 'love the stranger' (10:18-19) alongside herem genocide commands against Canaanites (ch.7,20).

T03.note: God is more systematically presented: clear rules, predictable consequences. Less capricious than earlier sources.

T04.note: Maximum moral order: blessings/curses (chs.27-28) directly tied to obedience. Retributive theology at its peak.

T05.note: D source balances: centralizes cult (priestly concern) but also emphasizes justice and prophetic authority (18:15-22).

T06.note: God causes all. No Satan. God even controls other nations as instruments of punishment.

T07.note: Blessings/curses entirely this-worldly: long life, prosperity, rain vs drought, defeat, exile. Zero afterlife.

T08.note: Some humanitarian improvements (rape victim not punished if in field, 22:25-27) but women still largely property. Captive-wife provisions (21:10-14).

T10.note: Maximally conditional. The entire theological framework is 'obey = blessed, disobey = cursed.'

T11.note: Strong free will: 'Choose life' (30:19). But God also hardens enemies.

T12.note: Maximally centralized: 'the place YHWH will choose.' All other worship sites to be destroyed.

T13.note: Moses as prophet-lawgiver supreme. Future prophets promised (18:15). Kings regulated (17:14-20). Priests receive Levitical dues.

T14.note: Both: 'treasured possession' (privilege) but also 'stiff-necked people' under constant obligation (burden).

T15.note: Both: warrior (herem commands) and provider (rain, crops, prosperity for obedience).

T16.note: Primarily legal, not mythological. Moses' speeches are rhetorical, not narrative.

T17.note: Actually revises Exodus laws (slave release, dietary exceptions, centralization) — law is evolving even as it claims to be Mosaic.

T18.note: Both: individual responsibility in some laws, but collective punishment for national apostasy (exile).

T19.note: Land as gift but requires conquest. Detailed instructions for warfare. Idealized boundaries.

conduct.diet: Dietary code restated (ch.14), largely matching Leviticus. New exception: non-sacrificial slaughter allowed far from sanctuary — 'as you would eat gazelle or deer' (12:15). Boiling kid in mother's milk prohibited (14:21).
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Captive women may be married with conditions (21:10-14). Rape of betrothed woman: man executed, woman not punished if in field (22:25-27). Levirate marriage codified (25:5-10). Cross-dressing prohibited (22:5).
conduct.slavery: Revised: slaves freed after 6 years WITH provisions — livestock, grain, wine (15:12-18). More humanitarian than Exodus version. Fugitive slaves must not be returned (23:15-16) — remarkably progressive.
conduct.clothing: No mixed wool-linen (22:11). Tassels on four corners of cloak (22:12). Cross-dressing prohibited (22:5).
conduct.agriculture: Sabbath year: debt release (15:1-6). No mixing seeds, no yoking ox and donkey together (22:9-10). Gleaning: leave sheaves for alien, orphan, widow (24:19-22).
conduct.economic_justice: Debt release every 7 years (15:1-6). No interest to fellow Israelites, but permitted to foreigners (23:19-20). Fair weights (25:13-16). Prompt payment of wages (24:14-15).
conduct.violence_war: Different rules for near vs distant cities (ch.20). Offer peace to distant cities first; if refused, kill males, keep women/children. Canaanite cities: total herem destruction. Limits on corporal punishment: max 40 lashes (25:1-3).
conduct.ritual_calendar: Three pilgrimage festivals: Passover, Weeks, Booths (16:1-17). Tithe system (14:22-29): third-year tithe for Levites, aliens, orphans, widows.

JOS.name: Joshua
JOS.dating: Core 7th c. DtrH, with P additions. Final form exilic.
JOS.sources: DtrH. Some older traditions. P in land allotments.
JOS.summary: Idealized conquest narrative that archaeology contradicts. Theological proof that YHWH fulfills promises. Essentially a land-claim document.

T01.note: 'Choose whom you will serve' (24:15) implies other gods are live options. Holy war assumes YHWH competing with Canaanite gods.

T02.note: Maximum exclusivism: herem genocide of Canaanite populations. Rahab the exception that proves the rule.

T03.note: God as strategic commander: detailed battle plans, walls fall on cue. But also surprised by Achan's sin.

T04.note: Mostly moral order (obey = victory) but Achan's family executed for his sin. Collective punishment.

T05.note: Neither strongly prophetic nor priestly. Joshua as military-covenant leader.

T06.note: God directly causes walls to fall, hailstones to kill, sun to stand still.

T07.note: No afterlife. Victory/death in this world only.

T08.note: Rahab shows female agency through cleverness. But women mostly invisible in conquest narrative.

T10.note: Conditional: land possession depends on covenant faithfulness. Achan's violation threatens everything.

T11.note: People choose (covenant renewal at Shechem) but God determines military outcomes.

T12.note: Ark as mobile center. Shiloh established but not exclusive.

T13.note: Joshua as unique leader — not clearly prophet, king, or priest.

T14.note: Election as maximum privilege: land given, enemies destroyed.

T15.note: Maximum warrior: God fights through miraculous violence.

T16.note: Mix: mythological elements (sun standing still, walls falling) with detailed geographical lists.

T17.note: Assumes Mosaic law as given framework.

T18.note: Heavily collective: Achan's whole family killed, entire Canaanite populations destroyed.

T19.note: Both simultaneously: land is divine gift AND requires brutal military conquest.

conduct.diet: No new regulations.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Rahab incorporated into Israel. No new marriage law.
conduct.slavery: No new regulations. Gibeonites made permanent servants through deception (ch.9).
conduct.economic_justice: No codes. Land allotment by divine lot.
conduct.violence_war: Herem warfare at maximum: Jericho (ch.6), Ai (ch.8), southern campaign (ch.10), northern campaign (ch.11). All living things destroyed in some cities. Hanging bodies displayed (8:29, 10:26-27).
conduct.ritual_calendar: Passover celebrated at Gilgal (5:10-12). Circumcision of new generation (5:2-9).

JDG.name: Judges
JDG.dating: Pre-monarchic traditions with DtrH framework (7th-6th c.). Song of Deborah (ch.5) possibly 12th-11th c.
JDG.sources: DtrH framework over older hero stories.
JDG.summary: Pre-monarchic tribal traditions within apostasy-oppression-deliverance cycle. Escalating moral chaos serves pro-monarchic argument.

T01.note: Israel repeatedly worships Baal and Asherah. YHWH-alone worship is the reform position, not the norm. Syncretism is the default.

T02.note: Tribal violence: civil war against Benjamin, massacre at Jabesh-Gilead. Foreigners are threat.

T03.note: God raises judges in response to crying out — responsive, not predetermined. But also withdraws protection capriciously.

T04.note: Sin cycle imposes order, but individual episodes show arbitrary suffering (Jephthah's daughter, Levite's concubine).

T05.note: Pre-institutional: neither prophetic nor priestly systems in operation.

T06.note: God sends oppressors as punishment. 'Evil spirit' from God between Abimelech and Shechem (9:23).

T07.note: No afterlife concepts.

T08.note: Deborah as leader (rare positive). But Jephthah's daughter sacrificed, Levite's concubine gang-raped and dismembered. Extreme gender violence.

T10.note: Conditional: sin cycle shows that apostasy triggers punishment, repentance triggers deliverance.

T11.note: People freely choose apostasy. But God sends the spirit on judges without their choosing.

T12.note: Distributed: worship at multiple sites. Micah's private shrine, Dan's stolen idol. No central sanctuary enforced.

T13.note: Charismatic (spirit-driven) leadership. Anti-monarchy tradition (Gideon refuses) coexists with pro-monarchy framing.

T14.note: Election as burden: constant failure, punishment, barely-functional deliverers.

T15.note: Warrior dominant: Ehud, Gideon, Samson, Jael. Violence is the solution in every cycle.

T16.note: Mix: clearly legendary elements (Samson's strength, 600 with jawbone) alongside plausible tribal conflicts.

T17.note: Mosaic law referenced in framework but characters violate it constantly without clear awareness of it.

T18.note: Mostly collective: whole tribes punished, whole tribes go to war. Benjamin nearly exterminated.

T19.note: Land partially possessed. Failure to complete conquest is the theological problem driving the narrative.

conduct.diet: Samson's Nazirite dietary restrictions (no wine, no unclean food) — which he violates.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Samson pursues Philistine women. Intermarriage with Canaanites presented negatively. Jephthah's daughter: human sacrifice implied. Rape and sexual violence (Levite's concubine). 400 virgins seized for Benjamin (ch.21).
conduct.homosexuality: Gibeah narrative (ch.19) parallels Sodom: men demand to 'know' the male guest. About gang rape, not consensual acts.
conduct.slavery: No new regulations.
conduct.economic_justice: No formal codes. Social breakdown is the theme.
conduct.violence_war: Charismatic warfare: spirit-driven judges. Ehud's assassination, Jael's tent-peg, Samson's jawbone. Escalating violence culminating in civil war. 'Devoted to destruction' language for enemies.
conduct.ritual_calendar: No formal calendar observed. Shiloh festival mentioned (21:19). General religious chaos.

RUT.name: Ruth
RUT.dating: Disputed: monarchic or post-exilic (5th-4th c.). May counter Ezra-Nehemiah's policies.
RUT.summary: Moabite woman becomes David's great-grandmother. Challenges ethnic exclusivism. Hesed as supreme virtue.

T01.note: God barely mentioned. No theological claims about divine nature.

T02.note: Maximum universalism: a Moabite — from a cursed people — embodies covenantal faithfulness and enters Israel's most important lineage.

T03.note: God works through providence (coincidence of Ruth gleaning in Boaz's field) rather than intervention.

T04.note: Moral order: hesed is rewarded. Faithfulness leads to restoration.

T05.note: Neither prophetic nor priestly.

T06.note: Not relevant.

T07.note: No afterlife. Naomi's concern is lineage continuity, not eternal life.

T08.note: Strong female agency: Ruth and Naomi drive the plot, navigate patriarchal system with shrewdness.

T10.note: Covenant framework not engaged.

T11.note: Human choices drive narrative; divine action is background.

T12.note: Not relevant.

T13.note: No institutional authority structures in play.

T14.note: Election not a theme.

T15.note: God barely appears.

T16.note: Realistic social narrative. No mythological elements.

T17.note: Implicitly challenges Deuteronomy 23:3 excluding Moabites from assembly.

T18.note: Individual: personal faithfulness and initiative determine outcome.

T19.note: Not relevant.

conduct.diet: No regulations.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Levirate-like obligation drives plot. Intermarriage with Moabite celebrated — directly contradicting Deuteronomy 23:3. Model of how inclusion works through loyalty.
conduct.slavery: No mention.
conduct.agriculture: Gleaning rights exercised: Ruth gleans in Boaz's field — Levitical provision for the poor in action.
conduct.economic_justice: Kinsman-redeemer system. Property redemption and levirate marriage as social safety net for widows. System works as intended.
conduct.violence_war: None. Entirely peaceful narrative.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Barley and wheat harvests provide seasonal setting.

1SA.name: 1 Samuel
1SA.dating: Multiple sources compiled during monarchy with DtrH editing.
1SA.sources: DtrH. Pro-monarchic and anti-monarchic sources preserved side by side. Separate 'History of David's Rise.'
1SA.summary: Transition from tribal confederation to monarchy. Both pro- and anti-monarchic traditions unresolved. David's rise as sophisticated political propaganda.

T01.note: Ark theology: YHWH's power is localized in object. Captured by Philistines but defeats Dagon. Henotheistic competition.

T02.note: Tribal: Philistines as permanent enemy. But Goliath narrative individual rather than ethnic.

T03.note: God regrets making Saul king (15:35). 'Evil spirit from the Lord' torments Saul. God responsive and changeable.

T04.note: Saul's disproportionate punishment (dynasty revoked for not killing all Amalekite livestock). David's sins overlooked.

T05.note: Samuel bridges prophet and priest. Prophetic authority to make/unmake kings established.

T06.note: 'Evil spirit from the Lord' on Saul (16:14). God directly causes tormenting. No external evil agent.

T07.note: Witch of Endor: Samuel's ghost rises from Sheol. Dead exist but in shadowy state. Necromancy works but is forbidden.

T08.note: Women mostly background. Hannah's prayer (ch.1) shows piety. Michal facilitates David's escape. But women as political prizes in marriage.

T09.note: David's rise: suspiciously cleared of guilt in every rival's death. Propaganda of innocence.

T10.note: Conditional for Saul (obedience required). Seeds of unconditional Davidic promise emerging.

T11.note: God chooses David, rejects Saul. But Saul and David also make real choices with consequences.

T12.note: Shiloh destroyed. Ark mobile. Multiple sacrifice sites (Samuel sacrifices at various locations).

T13.note: Prophetic authority supreme: Samuel makes and unmakes kings. Saul is punished for performing priestly sacrifice (ch.13).

T14.note: Israel requests king — seen both as privilege (like other nations) and rejection of God (burden/sin).

T15.note: Both: David as warrior (Goliath) and shepherd (literal shepherd boy). YHWH as warrior enabling David.

T16.note: Mix: historical-feeling political narrative with supernatural elements (evil spirit, Endor ghost).

T17.note: Mosaic law assumed but selectively applied. Samuel sacrifices outside central sanctuary without criticism.

T18.note: Both: Saul personally punished, but Amalekite genocide targets entire people.

T19.note: Land occupied but under threat. Philistine pressure drives narrative.

conduct.diet: No new regulations. Jonathan eats honey in violation of Saul's oath (14:27).
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Polygamy: David marries Michal, Abigail, Ahinoam, and more. Women as political currency. Michal given as prize then reassigned.
conduct.homosexuality: David-Jonathan relationship: 'your love to me was wonderful, surpassing the love of women' (2 Sam 1:26). Covenant language between them. Interpreted variously.
conduct.slavery: No new regulations.
conduct.economic_justice: No formal codes.
conduct.violence_war: Holy war: Amalekite herem (ch.15). Saul's failure to complete total destruction costs him the kingdom. David's guerrilla warfare and Philistine mercenary service.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Saul consults medium at Endor — necromancy explicitly forbidden but narratively functional.

2SA.name: 2 Samuel
2SA.dating: Core Succession Narrative possibly near-contemporary (10th c.). DtrH editing later.
2SA.sources: DtrH. 'Succession Narrative' (chs.9-20 + 1 Kings 1-2) considered early historical prose.
2SA.summary: David's reign with remarkable candor about failures. Davidic covenant establishes unconditional dynasty promise. Succession Narrative as sophisticated political writing.

T01.note: Mostly assumes YHWH-only framework but other nations still have their gods.

T02.note: David's empire includes subject peoples. Philistines, Moabites subdued. Not exterminated — shift from Joshua.

T03.note: God incites David to census then punishes Israel for it (ch.24). Nathan's parable suggests God can be surprised. God's plan unfolds through messy human choices.

T04.note: Nathan confronts David — moral accountability. But punishment falls on the child, not David directly. 70,000 die for census.

T05.note: Nathan as prophetic voice confronting royal power. But David also performs priestly functions (dancing before Ark).

T06.note: God directly incites census (24:1), sends plague. God sends 'evil spirit' on Saul in 1 Sam. Maximum: God causes evil.

T07.note: David's dead child: 'I shall go to him but he will not return to me' (12:23) — Sheol, not heaven. No afterlife hope.

T08.note: Bathsheba taken by royal power (ch.11). Tamar raped by Amnon (ch.13). David fails to act. Women suffer within power structures.

T09.note: Maximum critique: Bathsheba, Uriah's murder, family dysfunction. Yet also Davidic covenant (ch.7). Both poles present.

T10.note: Davidic covenant (ch.7) is unconditional: dynasty 'forever,' regardless of individual kings' behavior. Major theological shift.

T11.note: David freely sins (Bathsheba) but God also determined David's rise. Tension unresolved.

T12.note: David brings Ark to Jerusalem — centralizing worship. But not yet exclusive.

T13.note: King (David), prophet (Nathan), and priest (Zadok) in creative tension. Nathan confronts king. David performs priestly acts.

T14.note: David's election: enormous privilege (dynasty, empire) but also enormous suffering (Absalom, family collapse).

T15.note: Both: warrior who conquers empire and shepherd of Israel (5:2). Also grieving father ('O Absalom, my son').

T16.note: Primarily realistic political narrative. Succession Narrative reads like modern political writing.

T17.note: David performs priestly acts (dancing before Ark) that Levitical law would restrict.

T18.note: David sins individually but 70,000 die for census. Child dies for David's sin. Individual sin, collective punishment.

T19.note: Land secured through empire. David's conquests establish idealized boundaries.

conduct.diet: No regulations.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: David's polygamy extensive: multiple wives plus Bathsheba. Amnon rapes half-sister Tamar. Absalom takes David's concubines publicly (power display). Concubinage as institution.
conduct.homosexuality: David-Jonathan: 'surpassing the love of women.' Ambiguous — deep covenant friendship or romantic love? Text doesn't clarify.
conduct.slavery: No new regulations.
conduct.economic_justice: No formal codes. Absalom exploits lack of judicial access (15:1-6).
conduct.violence_war: Empire warfare: Moab, Ammon, Aram, Edom conquered. Uriah killed through strategic placement. Joab's assassinations. Civil war (Absalom). Blood vengeance continues.
conduct.ritual_calendar: David dances before Ark. Worship becoming associated with Jerusalem.

1KI.name: 1 Kings
1KI.dating: Sources from monarchic period; DtrH compilation 7th-6th c.
1KI.sources: DtrH. Royal annals. Elijah traditions possibly independent.
1KI.summary: Solomon's reign and kingdom division. Each king evaluated by one criterion: YHWH-alone worship at Jerusalem. Elijah introduces prophet-vs-king conflict.

T01.note: Carmel contest: YHWH vs Baal as real competition (ch.18). 'Still small voice' revises storm-god imagery. Transitional.

T02.note: Solomon's cosmopolitan court vs Elijah's exclusivism. Foreign wives condemned for bringing foreign worship.

T03.note: God more systematic in DtrH framework. But Elijah's experience at Horeb is intimate, surprising.

T04.note: Strong moral order: DtrH framework evaluates every king. Sin = decline, faithfulness = prosperity.

T05.note: Elijah as fiery prophet vs temple establishment. Prophetic authority challenges royal. Both present.

T06.note: God sends lying spirit to Ahab's prophets (22:22-23). God directly orchestrates deception.

T07.note: No afterlife. Rewards and punishments in this life.

T08.note: Jezebel: powerful but demonized. Queen of Sheba: positive foreign female. Women in power mostly condemned.

T09.note: Solomon glorified (temple) and condemned (idolatry). David referenced as standard.

T10.note: Conditional: Solomon's kingdom splits because of idolatry. Davidic promise preserved for one tribe.

T11.note: Kings make choices but God also predetermines (tearing kingdom from Solomon). Both.

T12.note: Temple built = maximum centralization. Jeroboam's Dan/Bethel shrines = the paradigmatic sin.

T13.note: Elijah as prophetic authority confronting king. Prophet can call down fire, declare drought. Prophet > king.

T14.note: Kingdom divided — election carries consequences. Solomon's privilege becomes Israel's burden.

T15.note: Both: warrior (Carmel fire from heaven) and intimate ('still small voice').

T16.note: Mix: Carmel contest has mythological feel, but political narrative is realistic.

T17.note: DtrH retroactively applies centralization standard to pre-centralization period.

T18.note: Individual kings judged, but their sins cause national consequences.

T19.note: Solomonic empire at maximum extent. Land loss begins with division.

T20.note: Solomon as wisdom figure (ch.3). But wisdom doesn't prevent his downfall.

conduct.diet: No new regulations.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Solomon's 700 wives, 300 concubines. Polygamy at maximum royal scale. Foreign wives as political alliances AND source of religious corruption. Condemned as cause of kingdom's fall.
conduct.slavery: Solomon's forced labor (5:13-18, 9:15-22). Corvée labor sparks northern rebellion — Samuel's warning fulfilled.
conduct.economic_justice: Solomon's taxation and forced labor. Rehoboam's refusal to lighten burden causes split. Economic exploitation drives political crisis.
conduct.violence_war: Elijah slaughters 450 Baal prophets (18:40). Military campaigns. Assassination and coups (northern kingdom pattern begins).
conduct.ritual_calendar: Temple dedication (ch.8). Jeroboam creates rival festivals at Dan and Bethel.

2KI.name: 2 Kings
2KI.dating: DtrH, 7th-6th c. Final chapters near-contemporary with 586 BCE fall.
2KI.summary: Decline and fall of both kingdoms through DtrH lens. Josiah's reform as ideal but too late. Jerusalem's fall as defining trauma.

T01.note: Josiah's reform pushes toward exclusive YHWH worship. Hezekiah destroys bronze serpent. Moving toward monotheism but still fighting syncretism.

T02.note: Tribal: Samaritans rejected as corrupted. Foreign nations as divine instruments.

T03.note: God more predictable: DtrH framework. But also uses Assyria/Babylon as instruments in ways that seem strategic.

T04.note: DtrH moral order overall, but strained: Josiah's righteousness doesn't save Judah. Manasseh's sins irreversible. Framework breaking.

T05.note: Both: Elisha as prophet-miracle-worker. Josiah's reform involves temple priesthood.

T06.note: God sends Assyria and Babylon as instruments of punishment. God controls enemy nations.

T07.note: No afterlife. All consequences in historical time.

T08.note: Athaliah seizes throne (ch.11) — powerful but presented as usurper. Huldah the prophetess validates Josiah's reform (22:14-20). Rare positive female authority.

T09.note: David as retrospective standard. Not directly portrayed.

T10.note: Conditional failing: despite Josiah, judgment comes. System breaking down.

T11.note: Kings choose sin, but God has already determined punishment.

T12.note: Josiah's reform: maximum centralization. All non-Jerusalem worship destroyed.

T13.note: Prophets (Elisha, Huldah) and king (Josiah) collaborate on reform. Temple priests also involved.

T14.note: Election as burden: chosen people destroyed. Exile as ultimate consequence.

T15.note: Both: God destroys through empires (warrior) but Elisha heals, multiplies, protects (shepherd).

T16.note: Mix: Elisha miracles (floating axe, raising dead) alongside realistic political history.

T17.note: 'Book of the Law' discovered — implies it was lost/unknown. Law as rediscovered, not continuously practiced.

T18.note: Entire nations exiled for accumulated sins. Individual kings judged but collective punishment.

T19.note: Land lost: exile. The gift has been revoked due to disobedience.

conduct.diet: No new regulations.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: No significant new material.
conduct.homosexuality: Male temple prostitutes removed in Josiah's reform (23:7) — suggests cultic rather than orientation-based.
conduct.slavery: No new regulations.
conduct.economic_justice: No formal codes.
conduct.violence_war: Assyrian siege warfare. Sennacherib's army destroyed by angel (ch.19). Josiah killed at Megiddo. Babylonian conquest and deportation. Temple destroyed. Gedaliah assassination.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Josiah's Passover 'not kept since the days of the judges' (23:22) — implies festival had lapsed.

1CH.name: 1 Chronicles
1CH.sources: Chronicler. Uses Samuel-Kings as source with systematic revision.
1CH.summary: Retells Israel's history from post-exilic priestly perspective. David sanitized. Satan replaces God as census instigator.

T01.note: Moving toward monotheism. Satan emerges as separate figure.

T02.note: Genealogies define community boundaries.

T03.note: God more systematically predictable than in Samuel.

T04.note: Immediate retribution theology.

T05.note: Maximally priestly: Levitical worship dominates.

T06.note: Satan incites census (21:1) — replacing 'the Lord' from 2 Samuel 24. Key moment of evil externalization.

T07.note: No afterlife.

T08.note: Women almost entirely absent.

T09.note: Maximum idealization: Bathsheba affair, Amnon's rape, Absalom's revolt all omitted.

T10.note: Davidic covenant emphasized.

T11.note: God determines through providence.

T12.note: Temple as supreme center.

T13.note: Priestly authority maximized. David reimagined as liturgical innovator.

T14.note: Election as priestly privilege.

T15.note: God more distant; works through institutions.

T16.note: Genealogies and institutional records. No myth.

T17.note: Revises Samuel-Kings theology: Satan replaces God, retribution made immediate.

T18.note: Individual kings' fates match their piety.

T19.note: Land as divine gift under Davidic stewardship.

conduct.diet: No regulations.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: David's marriages listed without moral comment. No Bathsheba scandal.
conduct.slavery: No regulations.
conduct.economic_justice: David's offerings for temple.
conduct.violence_war: Wars mentioned but sanitized. Emphasis on worship preparation over military conquest.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Levitical divisions and worship schedules dominate (chs.23-26). Musical guilds established.

2CH.name: 2 Chronicles
2CH.summary: Judahite monarchy with immediate-retribution theology. Each king's fate directly matches piety. Ends with Cyrus decree — hope.

T01.note: YHWH-alone assumed. Foreign gods as threat.

T02.note: Judah-only focus. North dismissed.

T03.note: God predictable: obey=prosper, sin=suffer. Every time.

T04.note: Maximum moral order: immediate retribution. Manasseh repents and is restored (explaining his long reign).

T05.note: Temple centrality structures entire narrative.

T06.note: Less emphasis on divine causation of evil than Kings.

T07.note: No afterlife.

T08.note: Women mostly absent.

T10.note: Conditional: every king gets what they deserve.

T11.note: Kings choose faithfulness or sin. Results are predictable.

T12.note: Temple as sole legitimate worship site.

T13.note: Priestly/temple authority paramount.

T14.note: Election tied to temple faithfulness.

T15.note: God works through institutional blessing/punishment.

T16.note: Institutional records.

T17.note: Systematically revises Kings: adds repentance to Manasseh, adds reasons for Josiah's death. Law evolving.

T18.note: Each king individually judged in own lifetime.

T19.note: Land lost (exile) but Cyrus decree = hope of return.

conduct.diet: No regulations.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Jehoshaphat's alliance marriage with Ahab's family condemned.
conduct.slavery: No regulations.
conduct.economic_justice: Temple restoration funding. Hezekiah's reforms.
conduct.violence_war: Wars as divine judgment/protection. Angels fight for Judah. Battles determined by piety.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Passover celebrations under Hezekiah and Josiah emphasized as return to proper worship.

EZR.name: Ezra
EZR.dating: 4th c. BCE. Contains authentic Persian documents.
EZR.sources: Possibly same school as Chronicles. Aramaic documents may be authentic.
EZR.summary: Return from exile and temple rebuilding. Foreign marriage dissolution. Ethnic purity and Torah as community constitution.

T01.note: YHWH worship exclusive. Persian God invoked (by Cyrus) — accommodation to empire.

T02.note: Maximum exclusivism: foreign wives expelled, ethnic purity enforced.

T03.note: God works through Persian empire — behind the scenes.

T04.note: Moral order: exile was punishment, return is restoration.

T05.note: Priestly concerns dominate: temple rebuilding, purity.

T07.note: No afterlife.

T08.note: Foreign women and their children expelled. Women as pollution threat to community.

T10.note: Torah obedience as condition of restoration.

T11.note: Community must choose purity.

T12.note: Temple rebuilding as urgent priority. Centralization restored.

T13.note: Ezra as scribe: textual authority alongside priestly. Scribal power emerging.

T14.note: Return is privilege; maintaining purity is burden.

T16.note: Administrative records and letters. No myth.

T17.note: Torah applied more strictly than its own letter (broader foreign-wife prohibition than Deuteronomy specified).

T18.note: Collective: entire community must divorce foreign wives. Individual marriages dissolved for communal purity.

T19.note: Land returned as divine gift via Persian decree.

conduct.diet: No new regulations.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Forced dissolution of marriages with ALL foreign women (chs.9-10). Goes beyond Deuteronomy's specific-nations prohibition. Children expelled too. Ruth's inclusion directly contradicted.
conduct.slavery: No regulations.
conduct.economic_justice: Temple resources and Persian imperial support.
conduct.violence_war: No military narrative. Return is peaceful under Persian patronage.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Second temple dedicated. Passover celebrated (6:19-22). Festival calendar restored.

NEH.name: Nehemiah
NEH.dating: 4th c. BCE. Contains 'Nehemiah Memoir' — possibly authentic first-person.
NEH.sources: Nehemiah Memoir plus editorial additions. Part of Ezra-Nehemiah composition.
NEH.summary: Wall-building plus religious reform. Physical reconstruction as communal metaphor. Public Torah reading as birth of scripture-centered religion.

T01.note: YHWH worship assumed. No theological innovation.

T02.note: Exclusivist: opposition from Sanballat/Tobiah. Foreign wives condemned. Boundary enforcement.

T03.note: God works through political processes.

T04.note: Moral order: rebuild properly, maintain purity, receive blessing.

T05.note: Both: wall-building is political; Torah reading is textual/scribal authority emerging alongside priestly.

T07.note: No afterlife.

T08.note: Foreign wives condemned again (ch.13). Women as boundary-crossing threat.

T10.note: Covenant renewal: conditional commitment to Torah observance.

T11.note: Community chooses to commit to Torah.

T12.note: Jerusalem/temple as center. Wall defines sacred space.

T13.note: Nehemiah as governor (political), Ezra as scribe (textual), priests (cultic). Three-way authority.

T14.note: Remnant identity: both privilege (chosen to return) and burden (constant vigilance required).

T16.note: Memoirs and records. No myth.

T17.note: Torah read publicly and interpreted — interpretation becoming as important as text itself.

T18.note: Individual economic exploitation addressed (ch.5), but collective covenant commitment.

T19.note: Land restored but diminished — tiny province, not empire.

conduct.diet: No new regulations.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Foreign wives condemned again (13:23-27). Solomon invoked as cautionary tale. Intermarriage as ongoing crisis.
conduct.slavery: Nehemiah confronts debt-slavery among Jews (ch.5). Forces wealthy to release enslaved countrymen and return interest.
conduct.economic_justice: Major: wealthy Jews exploiting poor through interest and debt slavery (ch.5). Nehemiah forces reform. Tithe restoration for Levites. Practical economic justice.
conduct.violence_war: No military campaigns. Opposition through political intrigue, not battle.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Sabbath enforcement: gates shut, merchants threatened (13:15-22). Booths festival celebrated (8:14-18). Public Torah reading becomes central event.

EST.name: Esther
EST.dating: 4th-3rd c. BCE. Set in Persian period.
EST.sources: Independent diaspora novella. Hebrew text never mentions God.
EST.summary: Diaspora survival without God. Political skill replaces divine intervention. Purim origin story.

T02.note: Jews as distinct people within empire. Survival through ethnic solidarity. But also violent revenge on enemies.

T04.note: Justice through reversal: Haman hanged on own gallows. But 75,000 enemies killed — proportionality?

T07.note: No afterlife. Survival in this life is everything.

T08.note: Esther exercises real power through beauty and compliance, then decisive action. Vashti deposed for asserting autonomy.

T11.note: Human agency drives everything. No divine determination visible.

T16.note: Realistic court narrative. No mythological elements.

T18.note: Collective: entire Jewish people threatened with genocide, entire people saved, entire enemy cohort killed.

conduct.diet: Esther may have maintained dietary laws in court — text doesn't say.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Vashti deposed for refusing to display herself. Beauty contest selects Esther. Royal harem. Marriage to non-Jew not condemned (strikingly different from Ezra-Nehemiah).
conduct.slavery: Court eunuchs prominent. Imperial servitude assumed.
conduct.clothing: Royal garments and honors as power markers.
conduct.economic_justice: Haman's bribe to king for genocide permit. Mordecai elevated to power. Economic dimensions of court politics.
conduct.violence_war: Self-defense warfare: Jews kill 75,000 enemies in one day plus 800 in Susa (9:6-16). Celebration of violent revenge without moral qualification.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Purim established as annual celebration of deliverance (9:20-32). Feasting and gift-giving. Only Jewish festival not connected to Torah or temple.

JOB.name: Job
JOB.dating: 6th-4th c. BCE. Prose frame older than poetic dialogues. Elihu speeches likely later addition.
JOB.sources: Independent. Prose folk tale frame + sophisticated poetic dialogues by different author.
JOB.summary: Sustained assault on Deuteronomistic retribution theology. Friends defend orthodoxy, Job insists on innocence, God's whirlwind speech asserts power over justice. Multiple incompatible answers to suffering.

T01.note: 'Sons of God' in divine council — polytheistic residue. But YHWH is supreme.

T03.note: God makes a bet with Satan, overwhelms Job with power instead of answering his moral question. Evasive and manipulable?

T04.note: Maximum challenge to moral order. Innocent Job suffers. God's answer is power, not justice. Framework demolished.

T05.note: Neither prophetic nor priestly — wisdom tradition standing outside both.

T06.note: Ha-satan as divine council member — adversary role, not independent evil. Transitional: evil still within God's court.

T07.note: 'If a man dies, will he live again?' — answer is no. 19:25 textually corrupt, not clear resurrection hope.

T08.note: Job's wife: 'Curse God and die.' Only female voice, and it's one line. Daughters given inheritance in ending (rare).

T11.note: God determines Job's suffering for a wager. Job's free response is what's tested.

T15.note: Both: God as cosmic power (whirlwind) but the question is whether God is also just/caring.

T16.note: Prose frame is folk tale. Poetry is philosophical argument. Behemoth/Leviathan are mythological.

T18.note: Maximally individual: one man's suffering and one man's relationship with God.

T20.note: Central: wisdom sought but declared inaccessible (ch.28). Human wisdom cannot solve the God problem.

conduct.diet: No regulations.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: No significant material. Job's second set of daughters named and given inheritance (42:13-15) — unusual.
conduct.slavery: Job references slaves in his household (31:13-15) but argues for treating them justly because God made them too.
conduct.economic_justice: Job's self-defense (ch.31) catalogs ethical behavior: no exploitation of poor, care for widows/orphans, generosity to strangers. Represents highest ethical standard.
conduct.violence_war: None.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Sacrifices mentioned in frame story. No calendar.

PSA.name: Psalms
PSA.dating: Spans centuries: some possibly 10th c. (royal psalms), many post-exilic. Collection grew in stages.
PSA.sources: Multiple collections: Davidic, Asaph, Korah, Songs of Ascents, Hallel. Five-book division mirrors Torah.
PSA.summary: Israel's hymnal spanning full emotional range. Preserves theological diversity without harmonization. Royal ideology, wisdom theology, prophetic justice, raw questioning all coexist.

T01.note: Ps 82: God judges 'among the gods' who die for injustice. 'God of gods' language. But also 'YHWH is one.' Full spectrum.

T02.note: Both: 'Praise YHWH all nations' (117) alongside 'dash their infants against rocks' (137). Full spectrum.

T03.note: 'Why have you forsaken me?' (22) — God experienced as absent. But also sovereign king enthroned.

T04.note: Ps 1 (moral order: righteous prosper) coexists with Ps 73 and 88 (where it doesn't work). Full spectrum.

T05.note: Both: temple/worship psalms alongside prophetic-justice psalms (Ps 50: God doesn't need sacrifices).

T06.note: God causes both good and trouble. No Satan figure in Psalms.

T07.note: Overwhelmingly: 'in death no remembrance of you' (6:5). But hints in 16:10, 49:15, 73:24. Transition visible.

T08.note: Almost entirely male voice. Rare exceptions (Ps 131 possibly female?). Women barely present.

T09.note: David as idealized psalmist. Royal psalms celebrate Davidic dynasty.

T10.note: Both: conditional psalms (obey to prosper) and unconditional royal promises (Ps 89).

T11.note: Both: human agency in laments (choose to cry out) and divine sovereignty (God enthroned).

T12.note: Temple/Zion psalms (46, 48, 84, 122) emphasize Jerusalem. But also 'the heavens declare' — God beyond temple.

T13.note: Royal psalms (king), prophetic-justice psalms, temple-worship psalms (priestly). All three present.

T14.note: Chosen people celebrate (privilege) and lament (burden of exile, enemies).

T15.note: Full range: warrior ('break them with rod of iron,' Ps 2) and shepherd ('The Lord is my shepherd,' Ps 23).

T16.note: Mix: mythological imagery (Leviathan, divine council) alongside historical memory (Ps 78, 105, 106).

T17.note: Torah celebrated as eternal (Ps 119) but interpretive tradition growing.

T18.note: Both: individual laments and communal laments. Both individual and collective sin/punishment.

T19.note: Zion theology: land as divine dwelling. Exile psalms mourn loss.

T20.note: Ps 1, 19, 119: wisdom accessible through Torah. But Ps 73 struggles to understand. Both poles.

conduct.diet: No regulations.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: No regulations. Marital imagery in Ps 45 (royal wedding). 'Rejoice in the wife of your youth' echoed.
conduct.slavery: No regulations.
conduct.economic_justice: Strong: 'Father of orphans, protector of widows' (68:5). God champions the poor throughout. Unjust judges condemned (Ps 82).
conduct.violence_war: Full spectrum: holy war psalms, imprecatory psalms (109, 137), but also peace visions (Ps 46). Violence and peace coexist.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Temple liturgy assumed. Pilgrimage psalms (Songs of Ascents). Festival psalms (Hallel, 113-118).

PRO.name: Proverbs
PRO.dating: Collections from different periods. Final compilation post-exilic.
PRO.sources: Multiple collections: Solomon, 'the wise,' Agur, Lemuel. Part of international ANE wisdom tradition. Amenemope parallels.
PRO.summary: Mainstream wisdom theology: universe is morally ordered. Lady Wisdom as cosmic principle. International ANE borrowing shows Israel's sages in broader intellectual culture.

T02.note: Both: international wisdom tradition (universal) but 'strange/foreign woman' warnings (particular).

T03.note: God is orderly, predictable: wise behavior produces good results. No divine caprice.

T04.note: Maximum moral order: hard work=wealth, laziness=poverty, wisdom=life. Completely predictable.

T07.note: 'Righteousness delivers from death' (11:4) — probably means premature death, not afterlife.

T08.note: Lady Wisdom as powerful cosmic female figure. Proverbs 31 woman as capable economic agent. But 'strange woman' as threat.

T11.note: Strong free will: you choose wisdom or folly, and get predictable results.

T16.note: Practical observation. No myth (except Lady Wisdom, ch.8).

T18.note: Maximally individual: your choices determine your outcomes.

T20.note: Maximum accessibility: wisdom calls in streets, available to all who seek (chs.1-9).

conduct.diet: Warnings against gluttony and drunkenness throughout.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Extensive: marital fidelity celebrated. 'Drink water from your own cistern' (5:15). Adultery condemned as deadly folly. Proverbs 31 'woman of valor' as ideal wife/economic agent. Prostitution warned against.
conduct.slavery: Slaves mentioned as household members. Fugitive slave protection echoed.
conduct.agriculture: Agricultural imagery throughout as metaphors for wisdom/folly.
conduct.economic_justice: Strong: honest weights (11:1), generosity to poor (19:17), exploitation condemned (22:22-23). Wealth through dishonesty warned against.
conduct.violence_war: None. Conflict resolution through wisdom, not force.
conduct.ritual_calendar: 'Fear of YHWH' as beginning of wisdom, but no specific ritual calendar.

ECC.name: Ecclesiastes
ECC.dating: 3rd c. BCE. Late Hebrew. Solomonic attribution is fiction.
ECC.sources: Qoheleth. Orthodox editor added conventional ending (12:13-14).
ECC.summary: Bible's most radical text. Everything is hebel (absurdity). No afterlife, no reliable retribution. Editor tacked on orthodox conclusion contradicting entire book.

T03.note: God is inscrutable: 'no one can find out what God has done from beginning to end' (3:11). God as mysterious, distant.

T04.note: Maximum attack on moral order: righteous suffer, wicked prosper, 'time and chance happen to them all' (9:11).

T07.note: Maximum death-as-oblivion: 'the dead know nothing' (9:5). Humans and animals share same fate (3:19-20).

T08.note: 'I found one man among a thousand but not one woman' (7:28). Misogynistic? Or ironic? Debated.

T11.note: Human effort is futile: outcomes not determined by merit. Neither free will nor divine plan reliably works.

T16.note: Philosophical reflection, not narrative.

T17.note: Editor adds orthodox ending that contradicts the book. The text itself subverts conventional wisdom law.

T18.note: Individual: one person's existential reflection.

T20.note: Maximum unknowability: wisdom brings grief (1:18). 'Of making books there is no end' (12:12). Wisdom cannot solve the human condition.

conduct.diet: 'Go, eat your bread with enjoyment, and drink your wine with a merry heart' (9:7). Positive embrace of food/drink.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: 'Enjoy life with the wife whom you love' (9:9). Monogamous happiness recommended.
conduct.slavery: 'I bought male and female slaves' (2:7) — listed among achievements tried and found empty.
conduct.clothing: 'Let your garments always be white; do not let oil be lacking on your head' (9:8) — enjoy life.
conduct.agriculture: 'Cast your bread upon the waters' (11:1). Agricultural metaphors for risk and reward.
conduct.economic_justice: Observations: oppression exists (4:1), government corruption exists (5:8), but no prescriptions. Observational, not legislative.
conduct.violence_war: None. 'A time for war and a time for peace' (3:8) — detached observation.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Offerings mentioned cautiously: 'Guard your steps when you go to the house of God; to draw near to listen is better than the sacrifice offered by fools' (5:1).

SNG.name: Song of Solomon
SNG.summary: Erotic love poetry without God. Mutual desire celebrated. Canonization required allegorical reading.

T08.note: Maximum female agency: woman initiates, pursues, describes desire as actively as man. 'I am my beloved's.' Mutual.

T16.note: Realistic love poetry. No myth.

conduct.diet: Wine, spices, honey as sensual imagery.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Mutual erotic desire celebrated without shame. Woman as active desiring subject. No property/contract framing. Possibly pre-marital desire celebrated. Most sexually positive text in the Bible.
conduct.slavery: No regulations.
conduct.clothing: Disrobing as intimate imagery. No regulations.
conduct.agriculture: Vineyard, garden, orchard imagery = erotic metaphor throughout.
conduct.economic_justice: No regulations.
conduct.violence_war: None.
conduct.ritual_calendar: None.

ISA.name: Isaiah
ISA.dating: Three sections: First Isaiah (1-39, 8th c.), Deutero-Isaiah (40-55, exile ~540s), Trito-Isaiah (56-66, post-exilic).
ISA.sources: At least three authors spanning 200+ years.
ISA.summary: Three books spanning centuries. First: Assyrian-era judgment. Second: Bible's clearest monotheism and Suffering Servant. Third: post-exilic tensions. Most theologically sophisticated prophetic collection.

T01.note: Full arc: First Isaiah still henotheistic. Deutero-Isaiah achieves explicit monotheism: 'besides me there is no god' (45:5). The monotheism breakthrough.

T02.note: Both: Israel as 'light to nations' (universal) alongside judgment oracles against specific peoples (tribal).

T03.note: Deutero-Isaiah: God is sovereign over all history, using Cyrus. More systematically omniscient than earlier texts.

T04.note: Both: Suffering Servant suffers unjustly (challenges moral order) but Isaiah 1-5 assumes retributive justice.

T05.note: Maximally prophetic: 'I desire mercy not sacrifice' spirit. Social justice as God's primary demand.

T06.note: God creates both 'weal and woe' (45:7). But evil not personified.

T07.note: Isaiah 26:19 — early resurrection hint. 25:8 — death swallowed up. Transitional: death-as-oblivion being challenged.

T08.note: Daughter Zion imagery (feminized city). Women mostly metaphorical. But 'a woman will encompass a man' hints.

T09.note: Davidic messianic hope (9:6-7, 11:1-9). David idealized as future standard.

T10.note: Both: conditional judgment (repent!) and unconditional promise (remnant will survive, Davidic branch).

T11.note: Both: 'Choose justice' (free will) and God hardens hearts (6:10, determinism).

T12.note: Temple theology in First Isaiah. But Deutero-Isaiah: 'heaven is my throne, earth my footstool' — transcends temple.

T13.note: Prophetic authority dominant. Prophet speaks for God against kings and priests.

T14.note: Both: Servant as light to nations (privilege) and Servant who suffers (burden). Full spectrum.

T15.note: Full range: warrior ('day of YHWH,' 'treading winepress of wrath') and shepherd/comforter ('comfort, comfort my people,' 40:1).

T16.note: Mix: apocalyptic visions (chs.24-27) alongside concrete political commentary.

T17.note: Deutero-Isaiah radically revises earlier theology. 'Behold, I am doing a new thing' (43:19).

T18.note: Both: national judgment/restoration and individual Servant's suffering.

T19.note: Land as divine gift to be restored. 'New heavens and new earth' transcends physical territory.

conduct.diet: No new regulations. Fasting critiqued: 'Is not this the fast I choose: to loose the bonds of injustice?' (58:6).
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Marriage as metaphor for God-Israel relationship. Foreigners welcomed who 'join themselves to YHWH' (56:3-8).
conduct.homosexuality: No direct mention. Eunuchs welcomed (56:3-5) — remarkable inclusivity.
conduct.slavery: Oppression condemned. Release captives (61:1). Jubilee language.
conduct.clothing: Sackcloth for mourning. 'Garments of salvation' as metaphor.
conduct.agriculture: Vineyard of the Lord (ch.5) as metaphor for Israel.
conduct.economic_justice: Central: 'learn to do good; seek justice, rescue the oppressed, defend the orphan, plead for the widow' (1:17). Exploitation of poor condemned. Fasting without justice rejected.
conduct.violence_war: Both: divine warrior imagery (63:1-6, treading winepress) and peace visions ('swords into plowshares,' 2:4; wolf with lamb, 11:6-9).
conduct.ritual_calendar: Sabbath keeping endorsed (56:2, 58:13). New moon and Sabbath mentioned. Fasting critiqued unless paired with justice.

JER.name: Jeremiah
JER.dating: Late 7th - early 6th c. LXX significantly shorter than MT.
JER.sources: Authentic oracles, Baruch's narrative, D prose sermons, editorial additions.
JER.summary: Judah's final decades. New covenant prophecy points beyond institutional religion. Confessions reveal prophetic anguish. Pro-Babylon political stance.

T01.note: YHWH supreme. 'Queen of Heaven' conflict reveals goddess worship still active. Monotheism not yet settled in practice.

T02.note: Both: judgment on nations (tribal) but also 'seek welfare of the city where I have sent you' (29:7) — diaspora universalism.

T03.note: God 'deceived' Jeremiah (20:7). Conditional prophecy (repentance changes outcome) suggests God responds, not predetermined.

T04.note: Both: conditional judgment (moral order if you repent) but exile seems inevitable regardless.

T05.note: Maximum prophetic: temple sermon (ch.7) declares temple provides no protection. Cult without justice is worthless.

T06.note: God brings Babylon as punishment. God causes the disaster.

T07.note: No afterlife. All judgment historical/national.

T08.note: Queen of Heaven worshippers are women defending their practice (44:15-19). Women have religious agency, though condemned.

T09.note: Davidic hope: 'righteous Branch' (23:5-6). David as future standard.

T10.note: New covenant (31:31-34): law on hearts, not stone. Moves beyond conditional compliance toward transformed relationship.

T11.note: Both: 'choose life' conditionalism alongside God's determined judgment that cannot be averted.

T12.note: Temple sermon undermines temple. But doesn't promote alternatives — just strips false security.

T13.note: Maximum prophetic authority vs royal power. Jeremiah against kings, false prophets, priests. Prophet stands alone.

T14.note: Maximum burden: election means higher judgment. Jeremiah suffers for his calling. 'Cursed be the day I was born.'

T15.note: Both: God brings destruction (warrior) but also promises restoration and new covenant (shepherd/healer).

T16.note: Concrete historical context. Political commentary, not myth.

T17.note: New covenant explicitly: old covenant 'broken' (31:32). Law must be internalized. Radical revision.

T18.note: Both: national exile (collective) and 'each shall die for his own sin' emerging (31:29-30).

T19.note: Land lost to Babylon. Jeremiah buys field (ch.32) as sign of future restoration. Both loss and hope.

conduct.diet: No new regulations. Rechabites praised for maintaining ancestral prohibitions (ch.35).
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Jeremiah commanded not to marry (16:2) — prophetic sign of coming destruction. Marriage metaphor for God-Israel (unfaithful wife).
conduct.slavery: Slave release demanded (ch.34). Slaveowners who re-enslaved freedmen condemned. Sabbath-year release enforced.
conduct.clothing: Loincloth and yoke as prophetic signs.
conduct.agriculture: No regulations. Vineyard and field metaphors.
conduct.economic_justice: Central: exploitation condemned. Slave release demanded. Shallum condemned for forced labor (22:13-17). 'Did not your father eat and drink and do justice?' (22:15).
conduct.violence_war: Babylon as divine instrument. Submission to Babylon counseled. Anti-war in immediate context. But oracles against nations (chs.46-51).
conduct.ritual_calendar: Temple worship critiqued when divorced from justice. Sabbath enforcement mentioned.

LAM.name: Lamentations
LAM.sources: Five acrostic poems, possibly multiple authors.
LAM.summary: Raw grief poetry after Jerusalem's fall. Oscillates between accepting deserved punishment and protesting its severity.

T03.note: God as enemy: 'bent his bow like an enemy' (2:4). God both source of suffering and only hope.

T04.note: Both: punishment deserved (1:18) and punishment excessive. Framework strained by trauma.

T06.note: God directly causes destruction. No external agent blamed.

T07.note: No afterlife. All suffering is now, in this life.

T08.note: 'Daughter Zion' as feminized suffering city. Maternal imagery: mothers eating children (2:20, 4:10). Gendered violence.

T10.note: Conditional framework assumed (we sinned, therefore punished) but questioned (is it proportionate?).

T12.note: Temple destruction is the crisis. Centralized worship destroyed.

T14.note: Maximum burden: chosen people utterly destroyed. Election as catastrophe.

T15.note: God as destroyer: 'he has become like an enemy' (2:5). Maximum warrior against own people.

T16.note: Concrete historical response. No myth.

T18.note: Entire community suffers. Collective punishment for collective sin.

T19.note: Land utterly lost. City destroyed. Maximum dispossession.

conduct.diet: Starvation described. Mothers eating children.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: No regulations. Sexual violence described: 'Women are raped in Zion' (5:11).
conduct.slavery: 'We have become orphans, fatherless; our mothers are like widows' (5:3). Enslavement lamented.
conduct.economic_justice: No prescriptions. Suffering of all social classes described.
conduct.violence_war: Siege warfare, starvation, massacre described in graphic detail.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Festivals have ceased: 'YHWH has abolished his appointed festivals' (2:6).

EZK.name: Ezekiel
EZK.dating: Early 6th c. Ezekiel among Babylonian exiles, ~593-571 BCE.
EZK.sources: Largely single author with editorial expansion. Unique priestly-prophetic synthesis.
EZK.summary: Strangest prophetic book: bizarre visions, performance prophecy, priestly regulation. God's glory leaves temple — theologically processing the unthinkable. Future temple vision never built.

T01.note: YHWH supreme, cosmic sovereignty. But elaborate angelic beings (ch.1) hint at complex divine realm.

T02.note: Both: nations judged (tribal) but restoration 'for the sake of my name' — not Israel's merit. Ambiguous.

T03.note: Systematic divine sovereignty: God acts 'for my name's sake' (36:22). Predictable: purity=blessing.

T04.note: Mostly moral order: individual responsibility (ch.18). But also God hardens hearts and uses destruction.

T05.note: Unique synthesis: Ezekiel is both priest and prophet. Priestly purity concerns combined with prophetic vision.

T06.note: God causes the exile. No external evil agent.

T07.note: Dry bones (ch.37): national resurrection metaphor that feeds literal resurrection theology. Transitional.

T08.note: Extended sexual-violence metaphors for Jerusalem/Israel as adulterous women (chs.16, 23). Most graphically misogynistic prophetic imagery.

T09.note: David as future shepherd (34:23-24). Idealized Davidic restoration.

T10.note: Both: conditional (sin caused exile) and unconditional (God will restore 'for my name').

T11.note: God determines: acts for divine reputation, not human merit.

T12.note: Ideal temple vision (chs.40-48): maximum centralization reimagined.

T13.note: Ezekiel as priest-prophet. Future vision gives equal authority to prince and priest. Dual authority model.

T14.note: Both: judgment for sins (burden) and promised restoration (privilege).

T15.note: Both: God as destroyer (sword of YHWH, fire) and as shepherd gathering scattered sheep (ch.34).

T16.note: Heavily visionary: throne-chariot, valley of bones, Gog and Magog. Most mythological/visionary prophetic book.

T17.note: Ideal temple vision revises Levitical regulations. New law for new age.

T18.note: Maximum individual: 'the soul that sins shall die' (18:4). Children do NOT bear parents' guilt. Major ethical advance.

T19.note: Land defiled, lost. But future tribal allotment described (chs.47-48). Restoration promised.

conduct.diet: No new regulations. Ezekiel's bread-baking with dung as prophetic sign (4:12-15).
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Extended metaphors of Jerusalem as sexually promiscuous woman (chs.16, 23) — graphic, violent imagery. Ezekiel's wife dies as prophetic sign (24:15-27).
conduct.homosexuality: Sodom's sin identified as pride, excess food, and neglect of poor (16:49) — NOT homosexuality. Significant reinterpretation.
conduct.slavery: No regulations.
conduct.agriculture: Land restoration imagery (ch.36). Future agricultural abundance promised.
conduct.economic_justice: Shepherds (leaders) condemned for exploiting rather than feeding the flock (ch.34). Prince's land allotment limited to prevent royal land-grabbing (45:7-9).
conduct.violence_war: Gog and Magog apocalyptic warfare (chs.38-39). But future vision is peaceful temple cult.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Ideal temple includes revised festival calendar (45:18-25). Modified Passover and Booths. New regulations for prince's offerings.

DAN.name: Daniel
DAN.dating: Final form ~165 BCE (Maccabean). Court tales (1-6) possibly 3rd c. Prophecies accurate until Antiochus IV, then wrong — vaticinium ex eventu.
DAN.sources: Two halves: court tales (1-6, partly Aramaic) and apocalyptic visions (7-12).
DAN.summary: Only fully apocalyptic OT book. Court tales model diaspora faithfulness. Visions introduce deterministic history, resurrection, Son of Man. Transition from prophetic to apocalyptic worldview.

T01.note: Moving firmly toward monotheism. But elaborate angelology (Michael, Gabriel) creates complex divine realm. Named angels are new.

T02.note: Both: Jews maintain identity in empire (particular) but God controls all kingdoms (universal sovereignty).

T03.note: Maximum sovereignty: God controls all history according to predetermined plan. Four-kingdom sequence predetermined.

T04.note: Moral order: faithful are protected (lion's den, furnace). But Maccabean martyrs die — requiring afterlife justice.

T06.note: Angelic warfare: Michael fights against national angels of Persia/Greece (10:13, 20-21). Evil becoming externalized in cosmic conflict.

T07.note: Maximum resurrection: 'Many who sleep in dust shall awake, some to everlasting life, some to shame' (12:2). The breakthrough.

T08.note: Women largely absent. Susanna (addition) shows female virtue under threat.

T10.note: Conditional in court tales (faithfulness rewarded). Apocalyptic visions: predetermined divine plan.

T11.note: Maximum determinism: history follows divine plan. 'A time is appointed' (11:35). Individuals faithful within predetermined framework.

T14.note: Both: God protects faithful (court tales = privilege) and faithful suffer martyrdom (apocalyptic = burden).

T15.note: Both: warrior (smashing statue, destroying beasts) and deliverer (saving from furnace, lion's den).

T16.note: Maximum mythological: beasts from sea, cosmic battles, heavenly court. Furthest from historical realism.

T17.note: Apocalyptic represents new theological framework. Old prophetic categories revised.

T18.note: Individual faithfulness: Daniel, three youths. But national destiny also predetermined.

T20.note: 'Those who are wise shall shine like the brightness of the sky' (12:3). Wisdom = faithfulness. But God's plan is mysterious.

conduct.diet: Daniel refuses king's food, eats vegetables and water (ch.1). Dietary law as diaspora identity marker. Purity maintained in hostile environment.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: No significant material.
conduct.slavery: Court service under foreign king — not slavery but constrained service.
conduct.economic_justice: No formal codes. Antiochus's plunder condemned.
conduct.violence_war: Apocalyptic warfare: cosmic battles between angelic forces. Antiochus as supreme villain. But 'not by human hand' (8:25) — divine, not human, victory.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Temple desecration (abomination of desolation). Counting days until restoration (12:11-12). Time calculations become central.

HOS.name: Hosea
HOS.summary: Marriage to unfaithful wife as YHWH-Israel metaphor. Earliest sustained anti-Baal polemic. Theologically powerful but gender-problematic.

T01.note: Baal-YHWH competition explicit. Israel credits Baal for fertility. Henotheistic context.

T03.note: God emotionally volatile: raging, punishing, then 'alluring' Israel back. Relational, not systematic.

T04.note: Retributive: sin causes punishment. But love overrides judgment (ch.11). Tension within moral framework.

T05.note: 'I desire hesed, not sacrifice' (6:6). Maximum prophetic ethics.

T06.note: God directly punishes. No external evil agent.

T07.note: No afterlife. 'After two days he will revive us' (6:2) is national restoration metaphor, not individual resurrection.

T08.note: Wife/Israel stripped, shamed, blocked (2:3-6). God's 'love' expressed through controlling and punishing feminized people.

T10.note: Conditional: repentance can restore. But God's love may override conditionality (ch.11).

T11.note: People choose apostasy. God determines consequences but also relents.

T13.note: Prophetic authority maximum. Priest and king criticized.

T14.note: Election as burden: 'You only have I known... therefore I will punish' (echoes Amos).

T15.note: Both: warrior (punishing) and lover/healer (ch.11, 14).

T16.note: Marriage metaphor is literary device, not myth.

T18.note: National sin, national punishment.

conduct.diet: No regulations.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: Marriage metaphor dominates. Hosea marries 'wife of whoredom' (ch.1). Adultery as theological category. Gender violence normalized in metaphor.
conduct.slavery: No regulations.
conduct.agriculture: Fertility: grain, wine, oil as YHWH's gifts, not Baal's (2:8-9). Agricultural theology.
conduct.economic_justice: No formal codes.
conduct.violence_war: No military narrative.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Festivals condemned as occasions for Baal worship. 'I will put an end to all her mirth' (2:11).

JOL.name: Joel
JOL.summary: Locust plague as Day of YHWH. Spirit poured on all flesh — bridging prophetic and apocalyptic.

T02.note: Both: all nations judged (universal) and 'YHWH roars from Zion' (particular).

T03.note: God predictable: repentance brings restoration. Day of YHWH follows pattern.

T04.note: Moral order: locusts as punishment, restoration if repent.

T05.note: Both: communal repentance ritual (priestly) and spirit-outpouring on all (prophetic-universal).

T06.note: God sends locusts as judgment.

T07.note: Cosmic signs suggest eschatological horizon but no explicit resurrection.

T08.note: 'Your sons AND daughters shall prophesy' (2:28). Rare explicit inclusion of women in prophetic authority.

T10.note: Conditional: repent and God restores.

T11.note: Human repentance required. But God determines outcome.

T13.note: Spirit democratization: all flesh prophesies. Undermines prophetic monopoly.

T14.note: Both.

T15.note: Warrior in Day of YHWH imagery. Restorer/healer in restoration promises.

T16.note: Cosmic apocalyptic imagery: sun dark, moon blood.

T18.note: Communal repentance, communal restoration.

conduct.diet: No regulations.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: No regulations.
conduct.slavery: No regulations.
conduct.clothing: 'Rend your hearts and not your garments' (2:13).
conduct.agriculture: Locust destruction and agricultural restoration central.
conduct.economic_justice: No formal codes.
conduct.violence_war: Day of YHWH: cosmic battle imagery. Valley of decision.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Communal fast and assembly commanded (1:14, 2:15-17).

AMO.name: Amos
AMO.dating: ~760 BCE. One of earliest writing prophets.
AMO.sources: Authentic oracles. Hopeful ending (9:11-15) likely post-exilic addition.
AMO.summary: Fiercest social justice voice. Economic injustice condemned. Election means higher judgment, not privilege.

T01.note: Assumes YHWH controls all nations. Other gods not engaged. Practical monotheism emerging.

T02.note: Both: oracles against foreign nations (tribal framework) but also 'Are you not like the Cushites to me?' (9:7) — Israel not uniquely special.

T03.note: God systematically just: sin produces punishment.

T04.note: Strong moral order, but applied in service of justice: the righteous-prosper framework is used to condemn the prosperous who exploit.

T05.note: Maximum prophetic: 'I hate your festivals... let justice roll down like waters' (5:21-24). Ritual without justice rejected entirely.

T06.note: God brings punishment directly.
