T07.note: No afterlife. Day of YHWH is historical, not eschatological resurrection.

T08.note: 'Cows of Bashan' (4:1): wealthy women condemned for exploiting poor. Women visible as economic actors, though negatively.

T10.note: Maximum conditionality: 'You only have I known... therefore I will punish you' (3:2). Election as accountability.

T11.note: People freely exploit the poor. God's judgment is certain consequence.

T13.note: Prophet against king: Amaziah tells Amos to leave. Prophetic authority overrides royal sanctuary.

T14.note: Maximum election-as-burden: being chosen means stricter judgment, not protection.

T15.note: Warrior: Day of YHWH as darkness. Lion roars, earthquake. Destructive divine imagery.

T16.note: Concrete social commentary. Cosmic imagery in visions but grounded in real economics.

T18.note: National judgment for systemic injustice. But prophetic lawsuit addresses specific individuals.

conduct.diet: No regulations.
conduct.marriage_sexuality: No regulations.
conduct.slavery: Condemnation: 'They sell the righteous for silver and the needy for a pair of sandals' (2:6). Debt slavery condemned.
conduct.clothing: 'They lay themselves down beside every altar on garments taken in pledge' (2:8). Violation of Exodus 22:26-27.
conduct.economic_justice: Central: false weights (8:5), exploiting the needy (8:4-6), taking bribes (5:12), trampling the poor (5:11). Most specific economic critique in the Bible.
conduct.violence_war: No military narrative. Violence against the poor is the concern.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Festivals condemned: God hates them when unaccompanied by justice (5:21-23).

OBA.name: Obadiah
OBA.sources: Brief oracle. Shares material with Jeremiah 49.
OBA.summary: Concentrated oracle against Edom for betraying Judah. Brother-nation enmity and territorial reversal.

T02.note: Maximum tribal: Edom condemned, Israel restored. Nationalist revenge.

T04.note: Moral order: betrayal punished, faithfulness rewarded.

T07.note: No afterlife.

T15.note: Warrior: divine destruction of Edom.

T16.note: Concrete geopolitical.

T18.note: Edom collectively punished.

T19.note: Territorial reversal: Israel will possess Edom's territory.

conduct.violence_war: National revenge celebrated.

JON.name: Jonah
JON.summary: Satirical story arguing against exclusivism. God's compassion extends to Israel's worst enemy. Divine question receives no answer.

T01.note: God controls sea, fish, wind, plant — sovereign over all nature and nations. Moving toward universal monotheism.

T02.note: Maximum universalism: God cares about Nineveh (Assyria!). Pagan sailors and Ninevites respond to God better than the Israelite prophet.

T03.note: God changes mind: Nineveh repents, destruction averted. But this is presented positively — divine responsiveness as compassion.

T04.note: Moral order affirmed (repentance changes outcome) but mercy trumps strict justice — which frustrates Jonah.

T05.note: Prophetic, but satirizing the prophet. Anti-institutional.

T07.note: No afterlife.

T10.note: God's mercy is unconditional: even Nineveh can be forgiven.

T11.note: Both: Ninevites freely repent, but God also controls the fish, storm, plant.

T13.note: Prophet satirized. Prophetic authority undermined: Jonah is the worst character in his own book.

T15.note: Maximum shepherd: God cares about 120,000 people and 'many animals' (4:11). Compassion extends to cattle.

T16.note: Legendary elements: big fish, instant plant growth. Folk-tale register.

T17.note: Challenges Deuteronomistic certainty about God punishing enemies.

T18.note: Nineveh repents collectively; individuals not distinguished.

conduct.violence_war: Anti-violence: God's mercy averts destruction. The opposite of herem.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Fasting and sackcloth in Nineveh (even animals!).

MIC.name: Micah
MIC.summary: Rural prophet attacks urban corruption. 'Do justice, love mercy, walk humbly' distills prophetic ethics. Bethlehem messianic oracle.

T02.note: Both: nations stream to Zion to learn Torah (4:1-3) but also judgment on surrounding peoples.

T04.note: Moral order: injustice will be punished. Jerusalem built with blood will be destroyed.

T05.note: Maximum prophetic ethics: 6:8 reduces everything to justice, mercy, humility. Implicit rejection of complex ritual.

T07.note: No afterlife.

T08.note: Women not prominent.

T09.note: Bethlehem oracle (5:2): Davidic messianic hope from humble origins.

T10.note: Conditional: justice required.

T12.note: 'Zion shall be plowed as a field' (3:12) — temple centralization critiqued when corrupt.

T13.note: Prophet against urban elite. Prophets and priests who prophesy for money condemned (3:11).

T14.note: Election as accountability: Israel held to higher standard.

T15.note: Both: 'swords into plowshares' (4:3) peace vision alongside judgment as destroyer.

T16.note: Concrete social commentary.

T18.note: Both: individual leaders condemned and national consequences.

conduct.economic_justice: Central: 'Do justice, love mercy, walk humbly' (6:8). Leaders who 'build Zion with blood' condemned (3:10). Land seizure condemned (2:1-2).
conduct.violence_war: Peace vision: swords into plowshares (4:3). But also judgment violence.
conduct.ritual_calendar: 'With what shall I come before YHWH?' (6:6-8). Ritual offerings rejected in favor of ethical living.

NAM.name: Nahum
NAM.summary: Pure nationalism celebrating Nineveh's destruction. YHWH as vengeful warrior. No moral complexity.

T02.note: Maximum tribal: Nineveh's doom celebrated with relish. Compare Jonah's mercy toward same city.

T03.note: God as predictable avenger: sin (Assyria's) will be punished.

T04.note: Moral order: Assyrian cruelty justifies divine vengeance.

T06.note: God directly destroys.

T07.note: No afterlife.

T08.note: Nineveh feminized as 'prostitute' to be stripped naked (3:4-7). Sexual violence as divine punishment imagery.

T14.note: Election as privilege: God avenges his people's enemies.

T15.note: Maximum warrior: 'vengeance' and 'wrath' (1:2). Storm-god imagery at full intensity.

T16.note: Concrete geopolitical.

T18.note: Entire nation collectively destroyed.

conduct.marriage_sexuality: City as prostitute — gendered violence imagery.
conduct.violence_war: Celebrated: graphic battle imagery. 'Piles of dead, heaps of corpses' (3:3). Divine vengeance without restraint.

HAB.name: Habakkuk
HAB.summary: Prophet argues with God. Theodicy in geopolitical terms. 'The righteous shall live by faithfulness.'

T03.note: God uses wicked Babylon to punish less-wicked Judah — morally incoherent? Habakkuk challenges God directly.

T04.note: Central tension: if God is just, why use a MORE wicked nation as instrument? Moral order challenged.

T05.note: Prophetic: dialogue with God. Not priestly.

T06.note: God uses Babylon as instrument. God controls evil nations.

T07.note: No afterlife.

T10.note: 'Righteous by faithfulness' — trust as the condition.

T11.note: Human faithfulness required within God's inscrutable plan.

T13.note: Prophet challenges God directly. Prophetic authority includes arguing with divinity.

T14.note: Election as burden: Judah punished through Babylon.

T15.note: Theophany (ch.3): full storm-god warrior imagery. Pestilence and plague as divine attendants.

T16.note: Ch.3 theophany has mythological register.

T18.note: National judgment, but 'righteous by faithfulness' is individual stance.

conduct.economic_justice: Woe oracles against exploitation: 'Woe to him who builds his house by unrighteousness' (2:9-12).
conduct.violence_war: Babylon's violence condemned even though God uses it.

ZEP.name: Zephaniah
ZEP.sources: Prophetic oracles with later hopeful additions.
ZEP.summary: Day of YHWH as creation-reversal. Universal judgment then humble remnant. Anawim theology.

T01.note: Syncretism condemned: astral cults, Milcom worship. Still fighting for YHWH-only.

T02.note: Both: universal judgment (all earth) and particular remnant hope.

T03.note: God predictable: sin brings Day of YHWH judgment.

T04.note: Moral order: wickedness triggers cosmic judgment. Humility brings survival.

T06.note: God causes destruction directly.

T07.note: No afterlife.

T10.note: Conditional: humble remnant survives.

T11.note: Choice to be humble determines survival.

T14.note: Both: judgment (burden) on Israel and hope for humble remnant (privilege).

T15.note: Warrior: creation-reversal destruction. But also 'YHWH your God is in your midst' (3:17) — intimate presence.

T16.note: Cosmic: creation reversed (1:2-3). Apocalyptic scale.

T18.note: Universal-collective destruction with remnant distinction.

conduct.violence_war: Cosmic destruction imagery.

HAG.name: Haggai
HAG.summary: One message: rebuild the temple. Economic hardship blamed on neglect. Zerubbabel as messianic hope (unfulfilled).

T03.note: God predictable: no temple = no blessing. Cause-effect clear.

T04.note: Maximum moral order: drought directly caused by temple neglect.

T05.note: Maximum priestly: temple rebuilding as sole priority.

T07.note: No afterlife.

T10.note: Conditional: rebuild temple, receive blessing.

T11.note: Community must choose to rebuild.

T12.note: Temple as absolute center.

T13.note: Priestly concerns dominate. Temple = everything.

T14.note: Restoration as privilege if they comply.

T16.note: Practical exhortation, not myth.

T17.note: Traditional priestly framework assumed.

T18.note: Communal: whole community must rebuild together.

T19.note: Land returned but impoverished. Temple needed for full restoration.

conduct.diet: Purity query about contagion (2:11-13): holiness doesn't transfer by contact but uncleanness does. Priestly ruling.
conduct.agriculture: Drought, poor harvests attributed to temple neglect.
conduct.violence_war: 'I will shake the nations' — cosmic language for political upheaval.
conduct.ritual_calendar: Temple rebuilding is the ritual calendar issue.

ZEC.name: Zechariah
ZEC.dating: First Zechariah (1-8): 520-518 BCE. Second Zechariah (9-14): 4th-3rd c.
ZEC.sources: Two distinct sections by different authors.
ZEC.summary: Two books. First: night visions, Satan develops, dual leadership. Second: apocalyptic with messianic passages central to Christian interpretation.

T01.note: Moving toward monotheism. But angelic intermediaries proliferate. Divine realm becoming complex.

T02.note: Both: 'many nations shall join themselves to YHWH' (2:11) — universalism. But also judgment on nations.

T03.note: Systematic: visions follow divine plan.

T04.note: Moral order: Jerusalem restored because God chose it.

T05.note: Both: priestly concerns (Joshua the high priest, temple) and prophetic visions.

T06.note: Ha-satan accuses Joshua (3:1-2) — more developed than Job. Adversary becoming distinct malevolent figure.

T07.note: Second Zechariah: eschatological imagery, mourning for pierced one, living waters. Transitional toward resurrection context.

T09.note: Messianic: 'Branch' as Davidic figure. Humble king on donkey (9:9).

T10.note: Both: restoration promised (unconditional element) and justice required.

T11.note: God's plan unfolds through visions. Predetermined.

T12.note: Temple centrality. Jerusalem as cosmic center in ch.14.

T13.note: Dual 'anointed ones' (4:14): priest and governor. Shared authority model. Neither alone.

T14.note: Restoration as privilege.

T15.note: Both: eschatological warfare (ch.14) and 'living waters from Jerusalem' (14:8).

T16.note: Night visions, cosmic battles, eschatological transformation. Highly mythological register.

T17.note: Second Zechariah introduces new messianic and eschatological concepts.

T18.note: Both: national destiny and individual 'pierced one' mourned.

T19.note: Jerusalem as eschatological center. Land theology transcendent.

conduct.economic_justice: 'Render true judgments, show kindness and mercy, do not oppress the widow, the orphan, the alien, or the poor' (7:9-10).
conduct.violence_war: Eschatological warfare: nations attack Jerusalem, YHWH fights (ch.14). Mount of Olives splits. But also peace: 'Not by might nor by power, but by my spirit' (4:6).
conduct.ritual_calendar: Fasting questioned (7:5-7): 'Was it for me that you fasted?' Fasting should produce justice, not ritual credit.
